name: Addison's disease
presentation: Insidious fatigue, weakness, weight loss, anorexia — often months of vague symptoms before diagnosis
Postural hypotension and salt craving (mineralocorticoid deficiency)
Hyperpigmentation: sun-exposed areas, palmar creases, buccal mucosa, scars — caused by excess ACTH stimulating melanocytes (primary only)
Precipitant may unmask: surgery, infection, or steroid withdrawal triggering acute crisis
signs: Hyperpigmentation of skin creases, buccal mucosa, scars, nipples (primary Addison's only — absent in secondary)
Sparse axillary/pubic hair in women (adrenal androgen deficiency)

bedside: BP: postural drop — lying and standing BP
bloods: U&E: hyponatraemia (↓Na), hyperkalaemia (↑K), hypoglycaemia — classic triad
Short Synacthen test (SST): 250 mcg tetracosactide IM/IV; cortisol peak <550 nmol/L = adrenal insufficiency
9am cortisol: <100 nmol/L strongly suggests insufficiency; >450 makes it unlikely; 100–450 = do SST
ACTH level: elevated in primary (↑), low/normal in secondary — distinguishes cause
Adrenal autoantibodies (21-hydroxylase Ab): positive in autoimmune Addison's (commonest cause in UK)
imaging: CT adrenals: calcification suggests TB or haemorrhage; small glands = autoimmune; enlarged = infection/malignancy
MRI pituitary: if secondary adrenal insufficiency suspected (low ACTH)

mx: Hydrocortisone: 20–30 mg/day in 2–3 divided doses — larger dose in the morning (e.g. 10 mg on waking, 5 mg at 12 noon, 5 mg at 4pm) to mimic diurnal cortisol rhythm
Fludrocortisone: 50–200 mcg once daily — mineralocorticoid replacement; titrate to BP, electrolytes, renin
Patient education: MedicAlert bracelet, steroid emergency card, self-injectable hydrocortisone kit for crisis; never stop steroids abruptly
traps: Sick day rules: intercurrent illness → DOUBLE the hydrocortisone dose; fludrocortisone stays THE SAME — doubling fludrocortisone causes dehydration and electrolyte imbalance
Hyperpigmentation is only in PRIMARY Addison's (high ACTH stimulates melanocytes) — absent in secondary adrenal insufficiency (pituitary cause, low ACTH)
Autoimmune Addison's is associated with other autoimmune conditions — screen for thyroid disease, T1DM, vitiligo, pernicious anaemia (autoimmune polyglandular syndrome)
In secondary adrenal insufficiency: no mineralocorticoid replacement needed (aldosterone preserved, regulated by RAAS not ACTH)
If starting levothyroxine in a patient with undiagnosed Addison's → can precipitate adrenal crisis; always replace cortisol first if dual deficiency suspected
